title: The __init__ Method
content:
### The `__init__()` Method

`__init__()` is the **constructor** -- it runs automatically when a new object is created.

---

### Purpose

Initialize object attributes (set initial state):

```python
class Person:
    def __init__(self, name, age):
        self.name = name
        self.age = age

p = Person("Alice", 25)
# __init__ runs here
```

---

### Always Called Automatically

```python
class Person:
    def __init__(self):
        print("Person created!")

p = Person()  # Prints "Person created!"
```

---

### Parameters

You can pass arguments to `__init__`:

```python
class Rectangle:
    def __init__(self, width, height):
        self.width = width
        self.height = height
        self.area = width * height

rect = Rectangle(5, 3)
print(rect.area)  # 15
```

---

### Default Values

```python
class Person:
    def __init__(self, name="Unknown", age=0):
        self.name = name
        self.age = age

p1 = Person()
p2 = Person("Bob", 30)
```

---

> **Note:** The name `__init__` has double underscores on both sides. These are called **dunder** (double underscore) methods. They have special meaning in Python.
codeExample:
class Person:
    def __init__(self, name, age):
        self.name = name
        self.age = age
        print(f"Created {self.name}, age {self.age}")

p1 = Person("Alice", 25)
p2 = Person("Bob", 30)

print(p1.name)
print(p2.age)
